query: association between intraoperative precursor events and postoperative major adverse events in various surgical procedures

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Surgical Errors -- Function -- Prevention of surgical errors aims to minimize mistakes and adverse events that may happen during the perioperative period. Surgery is a complex, high-risk medical field requiring precise coordination and execution from the surgical team. However, errors can occur at various stages of the surgical process, including during preoperative planning, patient positioning, anesthesia administration, intraoperatively, and postoperative care. Such errors can lead to complications such as bleeding, infection, tissue and organ damage, and even death. By implementing measures to prevent surgical errors, healthcare professionals aim to enhance patient safety and improve the quality of care. These measures may include standardized checklists, team training, advanced technologies, and ongoing quality improvement initiatives.
retrieved_docs.content: The Joint Commission is an independent, nonprofit organization created in 1951 that accredits over 20,000 healthcare organizations in the United States. The mission of The Joint Commission is to provide quality healthcare, prevent patient harm, and improve patient advocacy. Following a review of sentinel event alerts related to wrong-site surgery in 1998, The Joint Commission made an effort to eliminate wrong-site surgery by creating National Patient Safety Goals and a Universal Protocol for Preventing WSPEs. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Sentinel Event -- Clinical Significance -- Required Response to a Reviewable Sentinel Event
retrieved_docs.content: One of the most critical surgical safety measures is the timeout process, which involves a pause before the surgery commences involving all team members. This process occurs before surgery begins with the patient inside the operating room to ensure that the correct patient, the correct procedure, and the correct site are verified to minimize mistakes. [7] This is part of the Universal Protocol that the joint commission established in 2004. Implementing pre-procedural time-out with all team members is an example of strong corrective action. Spinal surgery poses an additional problem as the correct level identification is beyond the Universal Protocol's limits. [14] It is recommended that the surgeon develops and implements a patient-specific protocol by which he can correctly identify the level to be operated on. Most of the techniques used are learned at the resident and fellow level; however, deficiencies during the training have been noted. [15] Specific training sessions on clinical guidelines and review of wrong-site surgery cases may help reduce its occurrence. [10] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Issues of Concern -- Popular Surgical Risk Prediction Models
retrieved_docs.content: Major adverse cardiovascular events are important determinants of postoperative morbidity and mortality in stable patients undergoing non-cardiac surgical procedures. [20] The incidence of perioperative cardiovascular events after major noncardiac surgery in the United States has recently been reported as 3%. [21] A Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) has been suggested to estimate the perioperative risk of a major cardiac event: cardiac death, nonfatal cardiac arrest, or nonfatal myocardial infarction. [22] This tool was developed within a cohort study of 2,893 patients and subsequently validated on 1,422 patients older than 50 years undergoing major non-cardiac surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Surgical Errors -- Introduction -- Adverse Event
retrieved_docs.content: An adverse event is a preventable or unpreventable injury directly caused by medical management and unrelated to any underlying medical condition of the patient. The injury sustained due to the adverse event does not meet the standard of a sentinel event. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Surgical Errors -- Introduction -- Sentinel Event
retrieved_docs.content: A sentinel event is an unexpected event, including loss of limb or death, that leads directly to severe psychological or physical harm. This type of medical error requires immediate investigation. Sentinel events include WSPEs, post-surgical and post-procedural retained foreign bodies, administration of incompatible blood products resulting in a hemolytic transfusion reaction, and treatment-related errors resulting in death, such as incorrect medication administration. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Issues of Concern -- Popular Surgical Risk Prediction Models
retrieved_docs.content: ACS NSQIP was created to collect high-quality standardized data on preoperative risk factors and postoperative complications from participating hospitals within the United States. Information from 393 hospitals and 1,414,006 patients was collected and successfully used to develop a universal surgical risk calculator to predict one of 9 adverse outcomes within 30 days after surgery based on 21 patient-related variables and the planned surgical procedure according to the Current Procedural Terminology code (CPT code). [30] A recently updated version of the calculator based on 3.8 million surgical procedures consists of 20 variables to predict 15 outcomes and is available online. This is the most accurate patient- and surgery-specific tool to estimate the operative risk and guide shared decision-making. [31] All patient-related risk factors and outcomes are listed in Tables 4 and 5, respectively. One of the unique features of the calculator is an option to adjust operative risk based on the surgeon's experience.

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative modification of the risk factors is the most effective way to prevent postoperative morbidity and mortality. Appropriate postoperative care can also diminish operative risk if contemplated before surgical treatment. After surgery, admission to a critical care unit may prevent postoperative complications more effectively than escalating postoperative care once complications develop. [5] Of note, the operative risk is the risk of both immediate and long-term adverse events. For example, evidence-based prevention of venous thromboembolism after surgical treatment of cancer often implies chemoprophylaxis that extends beyond discharge from the hospital. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Medical Errors (Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Reducing Procedural Errors and Surgical Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Implementing checklists, such as the World Health Organization Surgical Safety Checklist (WHO SSC), has been shown to reduce procedural complications on a global scale. [22] It positively impacted safety process measures such as objective airway evaluation, prophylactic antibiotic use, confirmation of patient identity and site of the procedure, and sponge counts post-procedure. Implementing a surgical checklist has also been shown to reduce surgical complication rates, wound infections, blood loss, and mortality rates. [22] The WHO SSC is universally recommended to prevent surgical site infections, ensure safer anesthesia, and reduce perioperative mortality. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The selection of patients for surgical treatment is still largely influenced by a surgeon’s personal experience and judgment. However, estimation of an individual risk/benefit ratio for a specific surgical procedure can help to more objectively adopt a nonoperative management strategy or select the best surgical procedure at the most appropriate point of time. This article aims to discuss the problem of operative risk, review common risk factors and available statistical models to predict adverse events after surgery, and speculate about some practical aspects of the operative risk assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Root Cause Analysis and Medical Error Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Surgical Errors
retrieved_docs.content: Wrong-site surgery is a major cause of medical errors that can be mitigated through various preoperative safety checkpoints and has been the subject of a sentinel event alert by the Joint Commission. [13] [34] These errors have most commonly been noted in orthopedic surgeries. [35] Risk factors include several surgeons involved in surgical care or transfers to another surgeon for patient care, multiple procedures on a single patient, time constraint pressures, and unique circumstances requiring unusual or special positioning during a surgical procedure. [34] Wrong-site surgeries can easily be mitigated by ensuring proper preoperative measures, such as labeling the correct surgical site with an indelible pen or distinctively marking the nonsurgical site before the surgery. Intraoperative radiography can also assist in aiding the correct surgical site during the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Events -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Medication errors are a major contributor to both preventable and unpreventable adverse events. Medication-related adverse events include adverse drug effects, drug interactions, prescription errors, and administration mistakes. [4] [5] [6] Such events occur in both inpatient and outpatient settings. Surgical and procedural errors also account for many adverse events. Surgery-related complications, including wrong-site operations and inadequate perioperative care, result in a substantial number of patient deaths each year. [7] Additional contributors include nosocomial infections, improper documentation, inaccurate orders or prescriptions, misdiagnosis, missed diagnosis, and premature hospital discharge. [8] [9] Early discharge may lead to adverse outcomes (eg, injury) that require readmission. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Surgical Errors -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Error reporting is intended to prevent future error occurrences. The Joint Commission requires healthcare agencies immediately report all sentinel events to allow for timely investigational analysis and response. Root cause analysis and failure mode effect analysis (FMEA) are two major analytical frameworks following error reporting. [18] Root cause analysis is an organized team process to identify the causes of an event that directly led to an undesired outcome, followed by solutions to prevent similar undesired outcomes in the future. The Joint Commission requires all healthcare institutions to perform a root cause analysis investigation following any reported sentinel events. [6] FMEA is a proactive, structured approach to identify and address the causes and effects of a potential problem before an adverse event occurs. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention of Surgical Errors -- Introduction -- Near Miss
retrieved_docs.content: A near miss is an unplanned event that almost occurred and had the potential to result in patient harm. A near-miss analysis enables healthcare systems to identify and modify protocols before patient harm occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Operative Risk -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: One of the main challenges in surgery has long been to elaborate safe, effective, and technically feasible procedures for the best possible outcomes. Continuous progress in science and technology combined with cumulative experience in the field have refined indications for surgical treatment, prompted standardization of modern surgical techniques, and established comprehensive protocols in perioperative care. The most complex interventions can now be implemented with significantly reduced intraoperative trauma by using modern instruments and minimally invasive approaches. Surgical procedures became faster and safer, but complications are still prevalent, even in experienced hands. [1] [2]

Collectively, the knowledge base depicts a structured network in which prior cesarean delivery, cervical incompetence, and other unspecified pelvic abnormalities increase the risk of pregnancy and labor complications. These complications encompass anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system events, cervical tears, hemorrhagic pelvic tumors, and potential fetal death, and they are organized under broader categories of unspecified obstetric indications and late‑effect sequelae, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing complex obstetric presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query “إنهاء الحمل وخطر الإجهاض (SMQ)” groups together related obstetric events, including the specific procedure “إنهاء الحمل (جراحي)” (surgical termination of pregnancy) and several hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy—namely hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. By linking these entities, the SMQ facilitates comprehensive monitoring and assessment of safety signals associated with termination of pregnancy and the associated risk of miscarriage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 666
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES includes Surgical incisions as a subcategory, indicating that the practice of making incisions is a fundamental component of operative and anesthetic care. Surgical incisions are clinically linked to several serious postoperative complications. Specifically, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction are both associated with the presence of surgical incisions, reflecting the risk of herniation and bowel compromise after incision closure. In addition, Surgical incisions are associated with the Arabic‑described conditions فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (a strangulated incisional hernia) and فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (an incarcerated abdominal hernia), further emphasizing that improper healing or technical issues with incisions can lead to hernias that become trapped or strangulated, requiring prompt medical attention. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate, monitor for, and manage incision‑related hernia complications to improve surgical outcomes.

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how specific amniotic cavity problems, early membrane rupture, cesarean delivery, missed abortion, anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system complications, fetal disorders, and termination‑related hemorrhagic conditions are interlinked with pregnancy complications and their late effects, ultimately influencing the risk of delivering a non‑viable infant and underscoring the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 701
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical incisions serve as a central concept linking a range of operative and postoperative conditions. Within the broader domain of OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES, surgical incisions are directly associated with several specific forms of incisional hernia. These include Incisional hernia with obstruction but no gangrene, Obstructed incisional hernia with gangrene, and Postoperative incisional hernia with obstruction, each representing a distinct clinical scenario in which the integrity of the abdominal wall is compromised after surgery. In addition to these English‑language descriptors, surgical incisions are also connected to Arabic‑language entities that describe similar hernia complications: فَتْقٌ بَطْنِيّ مُنْحَبِس (incarcerated abdominal hernia) and فتق شقي مُخْتَنِق (strangulated incisional hernia). Furthermore, surgical incisions have a noted relationship with بضع ال رحم, a term referring to uterine prolapse, indicating that incision‑related factors may influence or be relevant to pelvic organ support. Together, these associations underscore the importance of careful incision planning, vigilant postoperative monitoring, and comprehensive management of hernia‑related risks to optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad obstetric category that encompasses the specific outcome of فشل التوليد بالملقط (forceps delivery failure). Within this framework, الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is directly linked to فشل التوليد بالملقط, indicating that the failure represents a particular adverse event or subcategory of the forceps delivery procedure. Consequently, both الولادة بالملقط and its associated failure, فشل التوليد بالملقط, are situated under the larger grouping of other procedures that induce or assist delivery, highlighting their interrelated roles in assisted vaginal birth and the importance of recognizing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication represent a more specific subset within the broader category of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complications). Likewise, اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is also classified under مضاعفات الحمل, indicating that fetal disorders are considered a type of pregnancy complication. Together, these relationships show that both anesthesia‑related cardiac events during labor and fetal disorders are encompassed by the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, underscoring the need for careful obstetric monitoring and management of these distinct yet related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل) constitute a broad clinical category that encompasses a variety of maternal and fetal conditions. Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication, and Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are both considered narrower subsets of pregnancy complications and are classified under “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” These anesthesia‑related complications illustrate how specific iatrogenic events fit within the larger framework of obstetric morbidity. Fetal disorders (اضطراب جنيني) are linked to pregnancy complications as a child entity, reflecting the impact of maternal health on the fetus. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2227
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are identified as a more specific subset within the broader category of pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل). Fetal disorder (اضطراب جنيني) is also classified as a type of pregnancy complication, indicating that abnormalities affecting the fetus fall under the umbrella of complications that can arise during gestation. Premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) likewise belongs to the group of pregnancy complications, reflecting its significance in obstetric outcomes. Together, these entities illustrate how diverse obstetric and perinatal issues—ranging from anesthesia‑related central nervous system effects to fetal abnormalities and early membrane rupture—are organized under the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, which is essential for comprehensive clinical assessment and management.
